Janus-QL Specification

Living Document, 2 July 2026

Previous Versions:
_ORCHESTRATOR_PREVIOUS_BUILD_FULL_LINK_
Editor:
Kushagra Singh Bisen (Ghent University - imec)

To the extent possible under law, the editors have waived all copyright and related or neighboring rights to this work. In addition, as of 2 July 2026, the editors have made this specification available under the Open Web Foundation Agreement Version 1.0, which is available at https://www.openwebfoundation.org/the-agreements/the-owf-1-0-agreements-granted-claims/owfa-1-0. Parts of this work may be from another specification document. If so, those parts are instead covered by the license of that specification document.

Abstract

This specification defines the first public Janus-QL Core query language contract implemented by the current validated Janus system for hybrid live-historical RDF querying.

Table of Contents

  1. 1 Introduction
    1. 1.1 Language Positioning
  2. 2 Conformance
    1. 2.1 Janus-QL Core Conformance Class
    2. 2.2 Implementation Extensions
  3. 3 Query Model
  4. 4 Janus-QL Core Grammar
    1. 4.1 Grammar Validation Rules
  5. 5 Window Declarations
    1. 5.1 Live Stream Window
    2. 5.2 Historical Fixed Log Window
    3. 5.3 Historical Sliding Log Window
  6. 6 Window Blocks
  7. 7 RDF Stream and Log Data Model
    1. 7.1 Window Materialization
  8. 8 Evaluation Translation
    1. 8.1 WINDOW Block Evaluation
    2. 8.2 Historical-Only Nested Subquery Evaluation
    3. 8.3 RStream Output
  9. 9 Historical Subqueries
  10. 10 Evaluation Semantics
  11. 11 Examples
    1. 11.1 Valid Janus-QL Core Examples
    2. 11.2 Invalid Janus-QL Core Examples
  12. 12 Limitations
  13. References
    1. Normative References

1. Introduction

Janus-QL is an RSP-QL-inspired query language for evaluating queries over live RDF streams and historical RDF event logs within a single query.

This specification defines a minimal first public Janus-QL language contract that matches the current validated Janus implementation. It defines only the public query forms that are intended for external users.

This specification describes Janus-QL using:

  • live windows over ON STREAM;

  • historical log windows over ON LOG;

  • WINDOW <name> { ... } blocks inside WHERE; and

  • historical-only nested subqueries inside an outer WHERE.

This specification does not define legacy baseline syntax as part of the public language.

1.1. Language Positioning

Janus-QL adopts the RSP-QL idea that named windows can be declared over dynamic RDF sources and referenced inside query graph patterns. However, Janus-QL is not a complete implementation of RSP-QL. This specification specializes the named-window model for hybrid live-historical querying by distinguishing live stream windows, declared using ON STREAM, from historical log windows, declared using ON LOG.

Janus-QL reuses SPARQL-style SELECT queries and graph-pattern matching, but this specification defines only the supported Janus-QL fragment. This specification does not define Janus-QL as a complete SPARQL 1.1 extension or as inheriting the whole SPARQL 1.1 feature set.

SPARQL or RSP-QL features that are not explicitly defined in this specification are outside Janus-QL Core. A Janus-QL processor MAY support additional features as implementation extensions, but such behavior is non-normative.

2. Conformance

The key words MUST, MUST NOT, SHOULD, SHOULD NOT, and MAY in this specification are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 [RFC2119] [RFC8174] when, and only when, they appear in all capitals.

A Janus-QL processor conforms to this specification if it accepts every query form defined by this specification and rejects every query form that this specification marks as unsupported or invalid.

This specification currently defines one conformance class: Janus-QL Core.

2.1. Janus-QL Core Conformance Class

A Janus-QL Core processor conforms to this specification if it accepts the Janus-QL Core query forms defined by this specification and rejects query forms that violate the Janus-QL Core grammar, validation rules, data model, evaluation translation, or supported feature set.

Janus-QL Core consists of the following public language elements defined by this specification:

  • PREFIX declarations;

  • an optional REGISTER RStream <output> AS clause;

  • top-level SELECT queries;

  • FROM NAMED WINDOW declarations;

  • live ON STREAM [RANGE ... STEP ...] windows;

  • fixed historical ON LOG [START ... END] windows;

  • historical sliding ON LOG [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] windows, using the interval behavior defined by this specification;

  • WINDOW <name> { ... } blocks;

  • basic triple patterns;

  • FILTER expressions where supported by this specification;

  • GROUP BY, aggregate expressions, and HAVING where supported by this specification; and

  • historical-only nested subqueries where explicitly defined by this specification.

In particular, Janus-QL Core conformance requires that a processor:

  • accept the query forms defined by this specification for live-only, historical-only, and hybrid live-historical queries within the supported Janus-QL Core fragment;

  • evaluate WINDOW blocks and supported historical-only nested subqueries according to the data model and evaluation translation defined by this specification; and

  • reject unsupported or invalid forms such as undeclared WINDOW names, unsupported result-stream operators, unsupported SPARQL-style operators, and unsupported nested-subquery forms.

2.2. Implementation Extensions

This specification is intentionally minimal. A processor MAY accept additional SPARQL-like, legacy, experimental, or implementation-specific extensions, but such behavior is non-normative unless this specification explicitly defines it.

Acceptance of non-normative extensions does not expand Janus-QL Core and does not change Janus-QL Core conformance.

3. Query Model

Janus-QL Core consists of:

  • zero or more PREFIX declarations;

  • an optional REGISTER RStream <output> AS clause;

  • one top-level SELECT query;

  • zero or more FROM NAMED WINDOW declarations over ON STREAM and ON LOG sources;

  • one WHERE clause containing WINDOW <name> { ... } graph-pattern blocks;

  • optional top-level GROUP BY and HAVING clauses where supported by this specification; and

  • historical-only nested subqueries where explicitly defined by this specification.

A Janus-QL query MUST contain exactly one top-level SELECT clause and exactly one top-level WHERE clause.

A Janus-QL query MAY omit REGISTER RStream <output> AS when the query is executed as a historical-only query.

A query that registers a live output stream SHOULD use REGISTER RStream <output> AS.

A FROM NAMED WINDOW declaration introduces a window name that MAY be referenced later in the WHERE clause by a WINDOW <name> { ... } block.

The public window declaration forms are:

  • FROM NAMED WINDOW <w> ON STREAM <s> [RANGE <range> STEP <step>]

  • FROM NAMED WINDOW <w> ON LOG <l> [START <start> END <end>]

  • FROM NAMED WINDOW <w> ON LOG <l> [OFFSET <offset> RANGE <range> STEP <step>]

The following are outside Janus-QL Core unless explicitly defined in a later version of this specification: full RSP-QL conformance, full SPARQL 1.1 conformance, IStream, DStream, CONSTRUCT, ASK, DESCRIBE, SERVICE, property paths, arbitrary SPARQL subqueries, and mixed live-historical nested subqueries.

4. Janus-QL Core Grammar

This section gives a compact EBNF-style grammar for the Janus-QL Core surface syntax defined by this specification.

The grammar is intentionally conservative. It describes only the public Janus-QL forms defined by this specification and does not imply support for other SPARQL 1.1 or RSP-QL syntax.

JanusQLQuery ::=
    PrefixDecl*
    RegisterClause?
    TopLevelSelectQuery

PrefixDecl ::=
    "PREFIX" PrefixName ":" IRIREF

RegisterClause ::=
    "REGISTER" "RStream" IRIOrPrefixedName "AS"

TopLevelSelectQuery ::=
    SelectClause
    WindowDecl*
    WhereClause
    GroupByClause?
    HavingClause?

SelectClause ::=
    "SELECT" SelectItem+

SelectItem ::=
      Var
    | AliasedExpression

AliasedExpression ::=
    "(" Expression "AS" Var ")"

WindowDecl ::=
    "FROM" "NAMED" "WINDOW" WindowName WindowSource WindowSpec

WindowSource ::=
      "ON" "STREAM" SourceName
    | "ON" "LOG" SourceName

WindowSpec ::=
      "[" "RANGE" Duration "STEP" Duration "]"
    | "[" "START" Timestamp "END" Timestamp "]"
    | "[" "OFFSET" Duration "RANGE" Duration "STEP" Duration "]"

WhereClause ::=
    "WHERE" "{" GraphPattern* "}"

GraphPattern ::=
      WindowBlock
    | Filter
    | HistoricalSubquery

WindowBlock ::=
    "WINDOW" WindowName "{" BasicGraphPatternItem* "}"

BasicGraphPatternItem ::=
      TriplePattern
    | Filter

TriplePattern ::=
    Term Term Term "."

Filter ::=
    "FILTER" "(" Expression ")"

HistoricalSubquery ::=
    "{" NestedHistoricalSelectQuery "}"

NestedHistoricalSelectQuery ::=
    SelectClause
    WhereClause
    GroupByClause?
    HavingClause?

GroupByClause ::=
    "GROUP" "BY" Var+

HavingClause ::=
    "HAVING" "(" Expression ")"

Duration ::=
    NonNegativeInteger

Timestamp ::=
    NonNegativeInteger

WindowName ::=
    IRIOrPrefixedName

SourceName ::=
    IRIOrPrefixedName

IRIOrPrefixedName ::=
      IRIREF
    | PrefixedName

Term ::=
      Var
    | IRIREF
    | PrefixedName
    | Literal

Var ::=
      "?" Name
    | "$" Name

Expression ::=
    /* supported Janus-QL Core SPARQL-style scalar or aggregate expression fragment */

Janus-QL Core reuses SPARQL-style lexical forms for IRIREF, PrefixedName, Literal, Name, PrefixName, and Var.

For this version of the specification:

  • Duration values are integer millisecond values.

  • Timestamp values are integer millisecond timestamps.

  • The current validated Janus implementation parses both Duration and Timestamp as non-negative decimal integers.

The Expression non-terminal is intentionally left compact. In Janus-QL Core it covers the supported SPARQL-style expression fragment accepted by the current Janus processor in SELECT, FILTER, and HAVING, including aggregate expressions such as AVG(?v) and comparison expressions such as ?x > ?y.

Nested historical subqueries do not introduce their own FROM NAMED WINDOW declarations in Janus-QL Core. They reference WINDOW names declared by the surrounding top-level query, and every referenced window MUST be a historical ON LOG window.

4.1. Grammar Validation Rules

The following well-formedness conditions apply to Janus-QL Core queries:

  • A live ON STREAM [RANGE ... STEP ...] declaration MUST use a RANGE greater than 0.

  • A live ON STREAM [RANGE ... STEP ...] declaration MUST use a STEP greater than 0.

  • A fixed historical ON LOG [START ... END] declaration MUST use START < END.

  • A sliding historical ON LOG [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] declaration MUST use OFFSET >= 0.

  • A sliding historical ON LOG [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] declaration MUST use a RANGE greater than 0.

  • A sliding historical ON LOG [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] declaration MUST use a STEP greater than 0.

  • A sliding historical ON LOG [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] declaration MUST NOT use RANGE > OFFSET in this version of the specification.

  • A WINDOW <name> { ... } block MUST NOT reference an undeclared window name.

  • A query MUST NOT declare the same window name more than once.

The current validated Janus implementation already documents and tests the RANGE > OFFSET rejection for historical sliding log windows, the ON STREAM versus ON LOG distinction, and rejection of unknown window references. The remaining conditions above define the Janus-QL Core surface-language contract even if a particular processor reports the error during parsing, a later validation phase, or execution planning.

5. Window Declarations

Janus-QL defines the following public window declaration forms.

5.1. Live Stream Window

The following form declares a live stream window:

FROM NAMED WINDOW <w> ON STREAM <s> [RANGE <range> STEP <step>]

A processor MUST treat this form as a live window over stream <s>.

5.2. Historical Fixed Log Window

The following form declares a fixed historical log window:

FROM NAMED WINDOW <w> ON LOG <l> [START <start> END <end>]

A processor MUST treat this form as a historical window over RDF event log <l> with explicit start and end bounds.

5.3. Historical Sliding Log Window

The following form declares a sliding historical log window:

FROM NAMED WINDOW <w> ON LOG <l> [OFFSET <offset> RANGE <range> STEP <step>]

A processor MUST treat this form as a historical window over RDF event log <l> that is resolved relative to live evaluation time.

Design note: historical offset semantics. This note is non-normative.

Janus-QL Core currently keeps the historical sliding-window interpretation aligned with the validated Janus implementation. In this version, historical sliding windows use the implementation-aligned interval interpretation [T - OFFSET, T - OFFSET + RANGE].

In this version, the validation rule rejecting historical sliding windows where RANGE is greater than OFFSET remains part of the current validated Janus-QL Core behavior.

A future version of Janus-QL may consider an alternative offset-to-window-end interpretation, where a historical sliding window at evaluation time T denotes the half-open interval [T - OFFSET - RANGE, T - OFFSET). Under that interpretation, OFFSET would represent how far back the historical comparison window ends, and RANGE would represent the width of the historical window. This may be easier to explain for use cases such as comparing a live window against a one-hour historical window ending 24 hours ago.

This alternative interpretation is not Janus-QL Core behavior in the current version. Adopting it would require a future versioned semantic change and corresponding implementation updates.

For this form, RANGE MUST NOT be greater than OFFSET. Queries where RANGE > OFFSET MUST be rejected.

6. Window Blocks

A declared window is referenced inside WHERE by a WINDOW <name> { ... } block.

Example:

WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
  ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
}

A WINDOW <name> { ... } block MUST refer to a window name declared by a FROM NAMED WINDOW clause in the same query.

The graph pattern inside a WINDOW <name> { ... } block is evaluated against the materialized window graph selected by that declaration, as defined in § 7 RDF Stream and Log Data Model.

7. RDF Stream and Log Data Model

This section defines the abstract data model over which Janus-QL Core window declarations and WINDOW blocks are evaluated.

The current validated Janus implementation ingests timestamped RDF event records. In implementation terms, each record carries:

  • an integer millisecond event-time timestamp;

  • an RDF subject;

  • an RDF predicate;

  • an RDF object; and

  • an RDF graph identifier.

This specification refers to such a record as an RDF event.

Conceptually, an RDF event is written as (τ, q), where τ is an integer millisecond event-time timestamp and q is the RDF quad contributed by that event. Multiple RDF events MAY share the same timestamp.

A live RDF stream is a potentially unbounded sequence of RDF events.

A historical RDF event log is a finite, persistent sequence of RDF events that can be queried retrospectively.

The timestamp domain for Janus-QL Core is the set of non-negative integer millisecond event-time timestamps, consistent with § 4 Janus-QL Core Grammar.

7.1. Window Materialization

A window declaration selects a time-bounded subset of RDF events from a live stream or historical log.

For a declared window w and evaluation time T, let E_w(T) denote the selected RDF events whose timestamps fall within the bounds defined for w by this specification.

The selected events are materialized for graph-pattern evaluation as the contents of the referenced window. This specification writes that materialized window graph as G_w(T).

A pattern of the form:

WINDOW ex:w {
  P
}

is evaluated by matching P against G_w(T).

The name used in WINDOW <name> { ... } identifies the Janus-QL window declaration. It does not, by itself, denote an original RDF named graph from the input data.

The current validated Janus implementation materializes selected RDF events by converting them to RDF quads and then exposing the distinct resulting quads for query evaluation. Accordingly, Janus-QL Core window materialization has graph-set semantics in this version of the specification: if two selected RDF events contribute the same RDF quad, that quad is present only once in the materialized window graph seen by the query processor.

This specification therefore defines window membership in terms of timestamp-selected RDF events, but graph-pattern evaluation in terms of the distinct materialized window graph exposed after materialization.

Event ordering within a live stream or historical RDF event log does not by itself determine query results in Janus-QL Core, except where a later section explicitly defines behavior relative to evaluation time. Window membership is determined by timestamp bounds, not by incidental storage or ingestion order.

This example is non-normative.

Assume a historical RDF event log contains the following RDF events:

(1000, ex:s1 ex:hasValue "20" ex:g)
(1100, ex:s1 ex:hasStatus ex:ok ex:g)
(1150, ex:s1 ex:hasValue "20" ex:g)

For a historical window whose selected timestamp interval includes 1000, 1100, and 1150, the selected events contain three RDF event records. The materialized window graph seen by the WINDOW block contains the distinct RDF graph content:

ex:s1 ex:hasValue "20" .
ex:s1 ex:hasStatus ex:ok .

The repeated ex:s1 ex:hasValue "20" event contributes the same RDF quad twice, but it appears once in the materialized window graph because the current Janus implementation evaluates over distinct materialized quads.

8. Evaluation Translation

This section defines a conservative Janus-QL Core evaluation translation in terms of SPARQL-style graph-pattern evaluation over the materialized window graphs defined in § 7 RDF Stream and Log Data Model.

For a declared window w and evaluation time T, let G_w(T) denote the materialized window graph exposed by w.

For fixed historical windows declared using ON LOG [START ... END], G_w(T) is independent of T.

For live stream windows declared using ON STREAM [RANGE ... STEP ...], G_w(T) depends on T according to the live evaluation schedule.

For historical sliding log windows declared using ON LOG [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...], G_w(T) depends on T according to the historical sliding-window semantics defined in § 10 Evaluation Semantics.

Let EvalGraph(P, G) denote the SPARQL-style evaluation of graph pattern P over graph G, returning a multiset of solution mappings.

Let two solution mappings be compatible when they assign the same RDF term to every shared variable.

Let Join(Ω1, Ω2) denote the SPARQL-style join of two multisets of solution mappings Ω1 and Ω2 using compatibility on shared variables.

8.1. WINDOW Block Evaluation

A Janus-QL Core window block of the form:

WINDOW w {
  P
}

is evaluated at evaluation time T as:

Eval_T(WINDOW w { P }) = EvalGraph(P, G_w(T))

The result of a WINDOW block is therefore a multiset of solution mappings, even though the underlying materialized window graph has graph-set semantics as defined in § 7 RDF Stream and Log Data Model.

When a WHERE clause contains multiple WINDOW blocks, Janus-QL Core combines their solution multisets using SPARQL-style join over compatible solution mappings.

For example, if a WHERE clause contains:

WINDOW w1 { P1 }
WINDOW w2 { P2 }

then the corresponding graph-pattern result at evaluation time T is:

Join(EvalGraph(P1, G_w1(T)), EvalGraph(P2, G_w2(T)))

FILTER expressions are evaluated over the solution mappings produced by the surrounding graph pattern, following the supported SPARQL-style expression behavior defined by this specification.

GROUP BY, aggregate expressions, and HAVING are applied after graph-pattern evaluation over the resulting solution multiset, following the supported SPARQL-style aggregate behavior accepted by the current Janus-QL Core processor. This specification does not imply full SPARQL 1.1 aggregate conformance beyond the supported Janus-QL Core fragment.

8.2. Historical-Only Nested Subquery Evaluation

A historical-only nested subquery MAY reference only declared ON LOG windows.

Such a nested subquery is evaluated over the corresponding materialized window graphs G_w(T) for the referenced historical windows. The nested subquery produces a multiset of solution mappings.

That result multiset is joined back into the surrounding query using the same shared-variable compatibility used for joins among WINDOW blocks.

Accordingly, if an outer pattern produces a multiset Ω_outer and a supported nested historical subquery produces a multiset Ω_hist(T), then the combined result is:

Join(Ω_outer, Ω_hist(T))

Live-only nested subqueries are not part of Janus-QL Core and MUST be rejected.

Mixed live-historical nested subqueries are not part of Janus-QL Core and MUST be rejected.

8.3. RStream Output

When a query uses REGISTER RStream <output> AS, the processor emits the complete top-level result multiset for each evaluation time T at which the top-level query is evaluated.

IStream and DStream are outside Janus-QL Core.

This example is non-normative.

PREFIX ex: <https://example.org/>

SELECT ?sensor ?liveValue ?histValue
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:live ON STREAM ex:traffic [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:hist ON LOG ex:traffic [START 0 END 60000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:live {
    ?sensor ex:value ?liveValue .
  }
  WINDOW ex:hist {
    ?sensor ex:value ?histValue .
  }
}

At evaluation time T, Janus-QL evaluates the first WINDOW block over G_ex:live(T), evaluates the second WINDOW block over G_ex:hist(T), and joins the resulting solution mappings on the shared variable ?sensor.

9. Historical Subqueries

In Janus-QL Core, a nested SELECT block MAY appear inside the outer WHERE clause.

A nested subquery is supported only when every WINDOW referenced inside that nested subquery is a historical ON LOG window.

Live-only nested subqueries are not part of Janus-QL Core and MUST be rejected.

Mixed live-historical nested subqueries are not part of Janus-QL Core and MUST be rejected.

Supported historical-only nested subqueries are lowered to a materialized historical result and joined back into the outer query.

A supported historical-only nested subquery MAY reference more than one historical window.

A materialized historical subquery MUST project at least one anchor variable and at least one additional value. A nested historical subquery that projects only one variable MUST be rejected.

10. Evaluation Semantics

The semantics in this section operate over the RDF stream and historical RDF event log data model defined in § 7 RDF Stream and Log Data Model.

The graph-pattern and solution-mapping evaluation translation used by this section is defined in § 8 Evaluation Translation.

Live windows are evaluated relative to the live stream schedule associated with the registered or executing query.

Historical fixed log windows use the explicit [START ... END] bounds given in the corresponding FROM NAMED WINDOW declaration over a historical RDF event log.

Historical sliding log windows are resolved relative to live evaluation time T.

For a declaration of the form:

FROM NAMED WINDOW <w> ON LOG <l> [OFFSET <offset> RANGE <range> STEP <step>]

the current Janus implementation resolves the interval as [T - OFFSET, T - OFFSET + RANGE].

This specification defines that same interval for this version of Janus-QL Core.

Supported historical-only nested subqueries are evaluated as a historical materialization step whose result is joined back into the outer query during overall query evaluation.

11. Examples

11.1. Valid Janus-QL Core Examples

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor ?value
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }
}

This is a valid live-only RStream query because it uses a supported live ON STREAM window and a supported WINDOW block over that declaration.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

SELECT ?sensor ?value
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 86400000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }
}

This is a valid fixed historical-only query because historical-only execution MAY omit REGISTER and the window uses the supported ON LOG [START ... END] form.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor ?liveValue ?historicalValue
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 86400000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?liveValue .
  }

  WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?historicalValue .
  }
}

This is a valid hybrid live plus fixed historical query because it joins two supported WINDOW blocks over one live and one historical declaration using the shared variable ?sensor.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor ?value
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:previousHour ON LOG ex:stream [OFFSET 86400000 RANGE 3600000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }

  WINDOW ex:previousHour {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?oldValue .
  }
}

This is a valid hybrid live plus historical sliding query because it uses the supported historical sliding ON LOG [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] form with the current validated interval behavior defined elsewhere in this specification.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

SELECT ?sensor ?historicalAverage
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 86400000]
WHERE {
  {
    SELECT ?sensor (AVG(?oldValue) AS ?historicalAverage)
    WHERE {
      WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
        ?sensor ex:hasValue ?oldValue .
      }
    }
    GROUP BY ?sensor
  }
}

This is a valid historical-only nested subquery example because the nested SELECT references only the declared historical ON LOG window.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor ?value ?historicalAverage
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 86400000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }

  {
    SELECT ?sensor (AVG(?oldValue) AS ?historicalAverage)
    WHERE {
      WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
        ?sensor ex:hasValue ?oldValue .
      }
    }
    GROUP BY ?sensor
  }

  FILTER(?value > ?historicalAverage)
}

This is a valid hybrid query with a supported historical-only nested subquery because the nested SELECT references only the declared historical ON LOG window.

11.2. Invalid Janus-QL Core Examples

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

SELECT ?sensor ?value
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:missing {
    ?sensor ex:hasValue ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because ex:missing is not declared by any FROM NAMED WINDOW clause in the same query.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:w ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:w ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 60000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:w {
    ?sensor ex:value ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because Janus-QL Core does not permit the same window name to be declared more than once.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  {
    SELECT ?sensor (AVG(?value) AS ?liveAverage)
    WHERE {
      WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
        ?sensor ex:value ?value .
      }
    }
    GROUP BY ?sensor
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because a supported nested subquery may reference only declared historical ON LOG windows.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor ?liveValue ?histAverage
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 0 END 86400000]
WHERE {
  {
    SELECT ?sensor ?liveValue (AVG(?histValue) AS ?histAverage)
    WHERE {
      WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
        ?sensor ex:value ?liveValue .
      }
      WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
        ?sensor ex:value ?histValue .
      }
    }
    GROUP BY ?sensor ?liveValue
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because mixed live-historical nested subqueries are outside Janus-QL Core.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER IStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:value ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because IStream is outside Janus-QL Core.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER DStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:value ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because DStream is outside Janus-QL Core.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:connectedTo/ex:value ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because property paths are outside Janus-QL Core.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    SERVICE <https://example.org/sparql> {
      ?sensor ex:value ?value .
    }
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because SERVICE is outside Janus-QL Core.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [START 1000 END 1000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
    ?sensor ex:value ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because a fixed historical window requires START < END.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 0 STEP 30000]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:value ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because a live window requires RANGE > 0.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 0]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:value ?value .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because a live window requires STEP > 0.

PREFIX ex: <http://example.org/>

REGISTER RStream ex:output AS
SELECT ?sensor
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:liveWindow ON STREAM ex:stream [RANGE 60000 STEP 30000]
FROM NAMED WINDOW ex:historicalWindow ON LOG ex:stream [OFFSET 1000 RANGE 1001 STEP 250]
WHERE {
  WINDOW ex:liveWindow {
    ?sensor ex:value ?value .
  }
  WINDOW ex:historicalWindow {
    ?sensor ex:value ?oldValue .
  }
}

A Janus-QL Core processor MUST reject this query because the current Janus-QL Core historical sliding constraint requires RANGE <= OFFSET.

12. Limitations

DEFINE BASELINE and USING BASELINE are not part of the public Janus-QL specification.

The implementation may still contain legacy compatibility paths and internal baseline-related names. Those implementation artifacts are non-normative and do not define the public language.

Older implementation paths may still accept historical [START ... END] and [OFFSET ... RANGE ... STEP ...] forms on ON STREAM. This specification does not define those forms as public Janus-QL syntax. In this version, historical windows are defined only over ON LOG.

Live-only nested subqueries and mixed live-historical nested subqueries are not part of Janus-QL Core.

SPARQL and RSP-QL features that are not explicitly listed in this specification are outside Janus-QL Core. This includes full RSP-QL conformance, full SPARQL 1.1 conformance, IStream, DStream, CONSTRUCT, ASK, DESCRIBE, SERVICE, property paths, arbitrary SPARQL subqueries, mixed live-historical nested subqueries, and other unsupported query forms beyond the public syntax defined here.

This specification does not define unsupported future syntax beyond the public forms listed in this document.

References

Normative References

[RFC2119]
S. Bradner. Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels. March 1997. Best Current Practice. URL: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2119
[RFC8174]
B. Leiba. Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words. May 2017. Best Current Practice. URL: https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc8174/
